examples.explanation: Step 1: Graph adjacency: {0:[1], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,3], 3:[2]}. Step 2: Test vertex 0: Remove 0, start DFS from 1. DFS visits: 1→2→3. Visited count=3 (V-1=3). Not articulation. Step 3: Test vertex 1: Remove 1, start from 0. DFS visits: 0 only (cannot reach 2,3). Count=1 < 3. Vertex 1 is articulation. Step 4: Test vertex 2: Remove 2, start from 0. DFS visits: 0→1 only (cannot reach 3). Count=2 < 3. Vertex 2 is articulation. Step 5: Test vertex 3: Remove 3, start from 0. DFS visits: 0→1→2. Count=3. Not articulation. Result: [1,2].

Step 1: Graph is a cycle: {0:[1,4], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,3], 3:[2,4], 4:[3,0]}. Step 2: Test each vertex: For vertex 0: Remove 0, start from 1. DFS visits: 1→2→3→4. Count=4 (V-1=4). Not articulation. Similarly for vertices 1,2,3,4: removing any vertex leaves a path connecting all others. All counts=4. No articulation points. Result: [].

approaches.name: Brute Force O(V*(V+E))
approaches.summary: Iteratively remove each vertex and check if the graph becomes disconnected using DFS or BFS. Use this for small graphs or to understand the problem intuitively.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Articulation Points Using Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of brute force approach to find all articulation points in an undirected graph.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to finding articulation points works by systematically testing each vertex to see if removing it disconnects the graph. An articulation point (or cut vertex) is a vertex whose removal increases the number of connected components in the graph. The intuition is straightforward: if removing a vertex causes the graph to become disconnected, that vertex is critical for maintaining connectivity. We iterate through all vertices V. For each vertex v, we temporarily remove it from consideration and perform a DFS/BFS from any remaining vertex. If we cannot visit all remaining vertices, then v is an articulation point. The algorithm: 1) Represent the graph using adjacency list. 2) For each vertex v in V: a) Mark v as removed (skip it in traversal). b) Choose any vertex u ≠ v as starting point. c) Perform DFS/BFS from u, counting visited vertices. d) If count < V-1 (since v is removed), v is articulation point. Time complexity is O(V*(V+E)) because for each of V vertices, we run DFS/BFS which takes O(V+E). Space is O(V+E) for adjacency list and visited array. Connection to approach: This is the complete implementation of the described brute force method. Common mistakes: Forgetting that the graph might be disconnected initially (need to handle multiple components), not properly skipping the removed vertex during traversal, and inefficient graph representation. Real code pattern: Use adjacency list, visited boolean array, and standard DFS with skip condition for removed vertex.

examples.trace: Step 1: Graph adjacency: {0:[1], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,3], 3:[2]}. Step 2: Test vertex 0: Remove 0, start DFS from 1. DFS visits: 1→2→3. Visited count=3 (V-1=3). Not articulation. Step 3: Test vertex 1: Remove 1, start from 0. DFS visits: 0 only (cannot reach 2,3). Count=1 < 3. Vertex 1 is articulation. Step 4: Test vertex 2: Remove 2, start from 0. DFS visits: 0→1 only (cannot reach 3). Count=2 < 3. Vertex 2 is articulation. Step 5: Test vertex 3: Remove 3, start from 0. DFS visits: 0→1→2. Count=3. Not articulation. Result: [1,2].

Step 1: Graph is a cycle: {0:[1,4], 1:[0,2], 2:[1,3], 3:[2,4], 4:[3,0]}. Step 2: Test each vertex: For vertex 0: Remove 0, start from 1. DFS visits: 1→2→3→4. Count=4 (V-1=4). Not articulation. Similarly for vertices 1,2,3,4: removing any vertex leaves a path connecting all others. All counts=4. No articulation points. Result: [].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check Connectivity After Vertex Removal
ladders.desc: Determine if removing a specific vertex disconnects the graph using DFS/BFS.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute force approach. Given a graph and a vertex v to remove, we need to check whether the remaining graph (with v excluded) is still connected. The intuition: if the graph becomes disconnected after removing v, then v is essential for connectivity. We perform a graph traversal (DFS or BFS) starting from any vertex other than v. During traversal, we skip v entirely—we don't start from it and don't traverse through it. After traversal, we count how many vertices were visited. If this count equals V-1 (all vertices except v), then the graph remains connected; otherwise, it's disconnected. Algorithm: 1) Input: graph adjacency list, total vertices V, vertex v to remove. 2) Initialize visited array of size V, all false. 3) Choose start vertex u: any vertex ≠ v (if graph has multiple components initially, may need to handle separately). 4) Perform DFS/BFS from u: in DFS, for each neighbor, if neighbor ≠ v and not visited, recursively visit. 5) After traversal, count visited vertices. 6) Return true if count == V-1 (connected), false otherwise (disconnected). Time: O(V+E) for one DFS/BFS. Space: O(V) for visited array. Connection to L0: This function is called V times in the full solution. Common mistakes: Not handling the case where the start vertex u might be in a different component than some vertices (but if graph was initially connected, this works). Also, inefficient neighbor checking. Real code pattern: DFS with parameter to skip vertex v.

examples.trace: Step 1: Adjacency: {0:[1],1:[0,2],2:[1,3],3:[2]}. Step 2: Remove vertex 1. Step 3: Start DFS from vertex 0 (≠1). DFS: visit 0 (neighbors: [1], but 1 is skipped). No further vertices. Visited: {0}. Count=1. Step 4: V-1=3. 1≠3, so return false (disconnected).

Step 1: Adjacency: {0:[1,3],1:[0,2],2:[1,3],3:[2,0]}. Step 2: Remove vertex 0. Step 3: Start DFS from vertex 1. DFS: visit 1 (neighbors: [0,2]; skip 0, go to 2). Visit 2 (neighbors: [1,3]; 1 visited, go to 3). Visit 3 (neighbors: [2,0]; 2 visited, skip 0). Visited: {1,2,3}. Count=3. Step 4: V-1=3. 3==3, return true (connected).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterative Vertex Removal and Connectivity Check
ladders.desc: The central technique of testing each vertex by removing it and checking graph connectivity.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the main algorithmic idea behind the brute force approach: for each vertex in the graph, temporarily remove it and check if the graph remains connected. The intuition is that an articulation point is defined as a vertex whose removal increases the number of connected components. By testing each vertex individually, we can identify all such points. The core logic involves: 1) Iterating through all vertices from 0 to V-1. 2) For each vertex v, simulate its removal by excluding it from graph traversals. 3) Check connectivity of the remaining graph (with V-1 vertices). 4) If disconnected, mark v as an articulation point. The key insight is that we don't actually modify the graph data structure; we just skip v during DFS/BFS. This is efficient in terms of implementation but computationally expensive. Step-by-step: Initialize result list. For v in range(V): a) Perform connectivity check on graph with v removed (using DFS/BFS from any vertex ≠ v). b) If check returns false (disconnected), add v to result. Time: O(V) iterations × O(V+E) per check = O(V*(V+E)). Space: O(V) for result and visited array per check. Connection to L1: This logic repeatedly calls the connectivity check sub-routine. Common mistakes: Not resetting visited array between iterations, or incorrectly choosing start vertex when v is the only vertex in a component. Real code pattern: Loop over vertices, call helper function, accumulate results.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize result=[]. Step 2: v=0: Remove 0, start DFS from 1. DFS visits: 1→2. Count=2, V-1=2. Connected, not articulation. Step 3: v=1: Remove 1, start from 0. DFS visits: 0 only. Count=1, V-1=2. Disconnected, add 1 to result. Step 4: v=2: Remove 2, start from 0. DFS visits: 0→1. Count=2, V-1=2. Connected, not articulation. Result: [1].

Step 1: result=[]. Step 2: v=0: Remove 0, start from 1. DFS visits: 1 only (no edges to 2,3). Count=1, V-1=3. Disconnected, add 0. Step 3: v=1: Remove 1, start from 0. DFS visits: 0→2→3. Count=3, V-1=3. Connected. Step 4: v=2: Similar to v=1, connected. Step 5: v=3: Similar, connected. Result: [0].

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Connectivity Check via DFS/BFS
ladders.desc: Determine if an undirected graph is connected using depth-first or breadth-first search.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is fundamental: checking whether all vertices in a graph are connected (i.e., there's a path between any two vertices). In an undirected graph, connectivity means that starting from any vertex, we can reach all other vertices via edges. We use DFS or BFS to traverse the graph from a starting vertex, marking visited vertices. After traversal, if the number of visited vertices equals the total number of vertices V, the graph is connected; otherwise, it's disconnected. The intuition: a single traversal should cover all vertices if they're all in one connected component. Algorithm: 1) Input: graph adjacency list and V. 2) If V==0, return true (empty graph is trivially connected). 3) Initialize visited array of size V, all false. 4) Choose start vertex (e.g., 0). 5) Perform DFS/BFS from start: recursively visit all neighbors, marking as visited. 6) Count visited vertices (or check all are true). 7) Return true if count==V, false otherwise. Time: O(V+E) for traversal. Space: O(V) for visited array. Connection to L2: This connectivity check is adapted by skipping a specific vertex to test articulation points. Common mistakes: Not handling graphs with multiple components initially (this check assumes we want to test connectivity of the entire graph). Also, stack overflow in DFS for large graphs. Real code pattern: Standard DFS with visited set and recursion/stack.

examples.trace: Step 1: Adjacency: {0:[1],1:[0,2],2:[1,3],3:[2]}. Step 2: Start DFS from 0. Visit 0 (neighbors: [1]). Visit 1 (neighbors: [0,2]; 0 visited, go to 2). Visit 2 (neighbors: [1,3]; 1 visited, go to 3). Visit 3 (neighbors: [2]; 2 visited). Visited: {0,1,2,3}. Count=4. V=4. 4==4, so connected.
Step 1: Adjacency: {0:[1],1:[0],2:[3],3:[2]}. Step 2: Start DFS from 0. Visit 0→1. Visited: {0,1}. Count=2. V=4. 2≠4, so disconnected (two components: {0,1} and {2,3}).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Graph Traversal (DFS/BFS) on Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Visit all vertices reachable from a start vertex using depth-first or breadth-first search.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves traversing a graph from a given starting vertex to visit all vertices that are connected to it via edges. DFS (Depth-First Search) explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking, using a stack (recursively or iteratively). BFS (Breadth-First Search) explores all neighbors at the present depth before moving to the next level, using a queue. The intuition: both algorithms systematically visit vertices to ensure we cover the connected component. For undirected graphs represented as adjacency lists, the process is: 1) Input: graph adjacency list, start vertex s. 2) Initialize visited array or set to track visited vertices. 3) For DFS: use recursion or stack. Push s, while stack not empty: pop vertex, if not visited, mark visited, push all unvisited neighbors. For BFS: use queue. Enqueue s, while queue not empty: dequeue vertex, if not visited, mark visited, enqueue all unvisited neighbors. 4) Continue until no more vertices to process. Time: O(V+E) as each vertex and edge is processed once. Space: O(V) for visited and stack/queue. Connection to L3: This traversal is used to check connectivity by counting visited vertices. Common mistakes: Forgetting to mark visited before processing neighbors (can cause infinite loops), or incorrect adjacency list representation. Real code pattern: DFS with recursion: def dfs(v): visited[v]=True; for neighbor in graph[v]: if not visited[neighbor]: dfs(neighbor).

examples.trace: Step 1: Start DFS from 0. Mark visited[0]=true. Step 2: Neighbors of 0: [1,2]. Visit 1: mark visited[1]=true, neighbors [0] (already visited). Backtrack. Visit 2: mark visited[2]=true, neighbors [0] (visited). Done. Visited set: {0,1,2}.

Step 1: Start BFS from 1. Queue: [1]. Step 2: Dequeue 1, mark visited. Enqueue neighbors 0,2. Queue: [0,2]. Step 3: Dequeue 0, mark visited, neighbor 1 (visited). Queue: [2]. Step 4: Dequeue 2, mark visited, enqueue neighbors 1 (visited),3. Queue: [3]. Step 5: Dequeue 3, mark visited, neighbor 2 (visited). Queue empty. Visited: {1,0,2,3}.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation and Basic Terminology
ladders.desc: Understand undirected graphs, vertices, edges, and adjacency list representation.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept introduces graphs, which are data structures consisting of vertices (nodes) and edges (connections between vertices). In an undirected graph, edges have no direction; an edge between vertex u and v implies u is connected to v and v is connected to u. Key terminology: Vertex: a point in the graph. Edge: a line connecting two vertices. Adjacency: two vertices are adjacent if there's an edge between them. Connected graph: there's a path between every pair of vertices. Component: a maximal connected subgraph. Articulation point: a vertex whose removal increases the number of components. Graph representation: Adjacency list is common: an array or dictionary where each vertex has a list of its adjacent vertices. For example, graph with vertices 0,1,2 and edges 0-1,1-2: adjacency list: {0:[1], 1:[0,2], 2:[1]}. Intuition: This representation efficiently stores neighbors for each vertex, making traversal easy. Step-by-step to build from edge list: Initialize empty list for each vertex. For each edge [u,v]: add v to u's list and u to v's list (since undirected). Time to build: O(E). Space: O(V+E). Connection to L4: This representation is used for DFS/BFS traversal. Common mistakes: Forgetting to add both directions for undirected edges, or using inefficient representations like adjacency matrix for sparse graphs. Real code pattern: graph = [[] for _ in range(V)]; for u,v in edges: graph[u].append(v); graph[v].append(u).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize lists for vertices 0,1,2: [], [], []. Step 2: Process edge [0,1]: add 1 to list of 0 → [1]; add 0 to list of 1 → [0]. Step 3: Process edge [1,2]: add 2 to list of 1 → [0,2]; add 1 to list of 2 → [1]. Final: 0:[1], 1:[0,2], 2:[1].

approaches.name: Tarjan's Algorithm O(V+E)
approaches.summary: Perform a DFS to assign discovery times and low-link values, identifying articulation points based on conditions in the DFS tree. This is the standard efficient method for undirected graphs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find All Articulation Points in an Undirected Graph
ladders.desc: Implement Tarjan's algorithm to identify all articulation points (cut vertices) in an undirected graph.
ladders.explanation: Articulation points are vertices whose removal increases the number of connected components in a graph. Tarjan's algorithm efficiently finds these points using a single DFS traversal. The intuition is that during DFS, we can track how 'deep' each vertex can reach back to earlier vertices. For each vertex u, we maintain discovery time (when it's first visited) and low value (the earliest discovery time reachable from u's subtree). A vertex u is an articulation point if: (1) u is the root and has at least 2 children in the DFS tree, or (2) u is not the root and has a child v where low[v] ≥ disc[u]. This condition means v cannot reach any vertex discovered before u, so removing u would disconnect v. The algorithm runs in O(V+E) time and O(V) space. Connection to L1: This level combines the DFS traversal with the articulation point detection logic. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle the root case separately, incorrectly updating low values, or not handling visited nodes properly in undirected graphs. Real code pattern: Initialize arrays for discovery times, low values, parent pointers, and visited status. Perform DFS from each unvisited vertex. During DFS, for each neighbor v: if v is not visited, recursively explore it and update low[u] = min(low[u], low[v]); check articulation condition. If v is visited and not parent, update low[u] = min(low[u], disc[v]). For root, check if it has ≥2 children.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start DFS from vertex 0. disc[0]=0, low[0]=0. Visit neighbor 1. Step 2: Vertex 1: disc[1]=1, low[1]=1. Visit neighbor 2. Step 3: Vertex 2: disc[2]=2, low[2]=2. Visit neighbor 3. Step 4: Vertex 3: disc[3]=3, low[3]=3. Visit neighbor 4. Step 5: Vertex 4: disc[4]=4, low[4]=4. No unvisited neighbors. Return to vertex 3: low[3]=min(low[3], low[4])=3. Check articulation: low[4]=4 ≥ disc[3]=3 → vertex 3 is articulation point. Step 6: Return to vertex 2: low[2]=min(low[2], low[3])=2. Check articulation: low[3]=3 ≥ disc[2]=2 → vertex 2 is articulation point. Step 7: Return to vertex 1: low[1]=min(low[1], low[2])=1. Check articulation: low[2]=2 ≥ disc[1]=1 → vertex 1 is articulation point. Step 8: Return to vertex 0: low[0]=min(low[0], low[1])=0. Root vertex 0 has only 1 child → not articulation. Output: [1,2,3].

Step 1: Start DFS from vertex 0. disc[0]=0, low[0]=0. Visit neighbor 1. Step 2: Vertex 1: disc[1]=1, low[1]=1. Visit neighbor 2 (via edge 1-2). Step 3: Vertex 2: disc[2]=2, low[2]=2. Visit neighbor 3. Step 4: Vertex 3: disc[3]=3, low[3]=3. No unvisited neighbors. Return to vertex 2: low[2]=min(low[2], low[3])=2. Check articulation: low[3]=3 ≥ disc[2]=2 → vertex 2 is articulation point. Step 5: Return to vertex 1: low[1]=min(low[1], low[2])=1. Check articulation: low[2]=2 ≥ disc[1]=1 → but vertex 2 is not child of 1 in DFS tree (parent of 2 is 1? Actually in DFS, from 1 we visited 2, so 1 is parent of 2. Wait, need to re-evaluate: In DFS from 0→1→2→3. For vertex 1: child is 2, low[2]=2 ≥ disc[1]=1 → condition holds, but is vertex 1 articulation? Removing vertex 1 does not disconnect graph because 0-2 edge exists. This is a mistake in condition due to back edge. Correct trace: At vertex 2, neighbor 0 is visited and not parent (parent is 1), so low[2]=min(low[2], disc[0])=0. Then low[2]=0. At vertex 1: low[1]=min(low[1], low[2])=0. Check articulation: low[2]=0 ≥ disc[1]=1? No, 0 < 1, so vertex 1 is not articulation. Similarly, vertex 0 is root with 1 child (since 0-2 is back edge, not tree edge). So only vertex 2 is articulation. Output: [2].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Traversal with Discovery and Low Values
ladders.desc: Implement a DFS that computes discovery times and low-link values for each vertex.
ladders.explanation: This DFS is the core of Tarjan's algorithm. The intuition is to explore the graph depth-first while tracking two key values per vertex: discovery time (disc) records when the vertex is first visited, and low value (low) represents the earliest discovery time reachable from that vertex's subtree (including back edges). During DFS, when visiting a vertex u, we set disc[u] and low[u] to the current time, then increment time. For each neighbor v of u: if v is not visited, we recursively explore v, then update low[u] = min(low[u], low[v]). If v is visited and v is not the parent of u in the DFS tree, we update low[u] = min(low[u], disc[v]) to account for back edges. This update captures cycles in the graph. The low value helps determine connectivity: if a vertex's child has low value ≥ its own discovery time, it indicates a cut point. Time complexity is O(V+E) as each vertex and edge is processed once. Space is O(V) for arrays and recursion stack. Connection to L0: This DFS provides the disc and low arrays needed to identify articulation points. Common mistakes include not handling parent pointers correctly (leading to incorrect back edge detection), or updating low with disc[v] instead of low[v] in certain cases. Real code pattern: def dfs(u, parent): set visited[u]=True, disc[u]=low[u]=time, time+=1. For each neighbor v in adj[u]: if not visited[v]: dfs(v, u); low[u]=min(low[u], low[v]). elif v != parent: low[u]=min(low[u], disc[v]).

examples.input: Graph: vertices 0,1,2 with edges 0-1, 1-2. Start DFS from 0.
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize time=0. Start at vertex 0: visited[0]=True, disc[0]=0, low[0]=0, time=1. Neighbors: 1. Step 2: Vertex 1 not visited. Call dfs(1, parent=0). visited[1]=True, disc[1]=1, low[1]=1, time=2. Neighbors: 0 and 2. For neighbor 0: visited and 0==parent? parent is 0, so yes, skip. For neighbor 2: not visited. Step 3: Call dfs(2, parent=1). visited[2]=True, disc[2]=2, low[2]=2, time=3. Neighbors: 1. Neighbor 1 visited and 1==parent? yes, skip. Return to vertex 1: low[1]=min(low[1], low[2])=min(1,2)=1. Return to vertex 0: low[0]=min(low[0], low[1])=min(0,1)=0. Final: disc=[0,1,2], low=[0,1,2]? Wait, low[2]=2, low[1]=1, low[0]=0. But in trace, low[1] became 1, not 0. Let's correct: At vertex 2, no back edges, so low[2]=2. At vertex 1, after dfs(2), low[1]=min(1,2)=1. At vertex 0, low[0]=min(0,1)=0. So low=[0,1,2]. However, in articulation points, low values might propagate. Actually, low[2]=2, but from vertex 2, can we reach earlier vertex? No back edges, so low stays 2. So output: disc=[0,1,2], low=[0,1,2].

examples.input: Graph: vertices 0,1,2 with edges 0-1, 1-2, 2-0. Start DFS from 0.
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at vertex 0: disc[0]=0, low[0]=0, time=1. Neighbors: 1 and 2. Step 2: Visit neighbor 1 (not visited). dfs(1,0): disc[1]=1, low[1]=1, time=2. Neighbors: 0 and 2. For neighbor 0: visited and 0==parent? yes, skip. For neighbor 2: not visited. Step 3: dfs(2,1): disc[2]=2, low[2]=2, time=3. Neighbors: 0 and 1. For neighbor 0: visited and 0!=parent (parent is 1), so low[2]=min(low[2], disc[0])=min(2,0)=0. For neighbor 1: visited and 1==parent, skip. Return to vertex 1: low[1]=min(low[1], low[2])=min(1,0)=0. Return to vertex 0: low[0]=min(low[0], low[1])=min(0,0)=0. Final: disc=[0,1,2], low=[0,0,0].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Identifying Articulation Points Using Low and Discovery Times
ladders.desc: Determine if a vertex is an articulation point based on low values of its children.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that a vertex u is an articulation point if removing it disconnects the graph. During DFS, this happens when: (1) u is the root of the DFS tree and has at least two children, meaning its removal splits the tree into multiple components. (2) u is not the root and has a child v such that low[v] ≥ disc[u]. This condition means that the subtree rooted at v cannot reach any vertex discovered before u via back edges, so removing u would disconnect v's subtree from the rest of the graph. The intuition is that low[v] represents the earliest vertex v can reach; if this is no earlier than u, then v's connectivity depends entirely on u. This check is performed during DFS after exploring each child. Time complexity for this logic is O(1) per edge, integrated into DFS. Space is O(1) extra. Connection to L1: This logic uses the disc and low values computed by DFS to make decisions. Common mistakes include applying the condition incorrectly for the root, or not counting children correctly for the root case. Real code pattern: In dfs(u, parent), after recursively exploring a child v, check if low[v] ≥ disc[u]. If true and u is not root, mark u as articulation point. For root, count children; if children ≥ 2, mark root as articulation.

examples.input: Vertex u=1, disc[u]=1, low[child v]=2, isRoot=false
examples.trace: Given: u=1, disc[1]=1, child v=2 with low[2]=2. Condition: low[v] ≥ disc[u] → 2 ≥ 1 is true. Since u is not root (isRoot=false), vertex 1 is an articulation point. This means removing vertex 1 would disconnect the subtree rooted at vertex 2.

examples.input: Vertex u=0, disc[u]=0, children count=1, isRoot=true
examples.trace: Given: u=0 is root with disc[0]=0. Children count=1. For root, articulation condition is: if children ≥ 2, then it is articulation point. Here children=1, which is less than 2, so vertex 0 is not an articulation point. Even if low values of children might satisfy other conditions, for root we only consider children count.

ladders.title: Building Block — Computing Low Values in DFS
ladders.desc: Update low values for a vertex during DFS using back edges and children's low values.
ladders.explanation: The low value of a vertex u is the minimum of: its own discovery time, the low values of its children in the DFS tree, and the discovery times of vertices connected via back edges (edges to already-visited vertices that are not the parent). The intuition is that low[u] represents the earliest vertex reachable from u's subtree, including through back edges that create cycles. During DFS, after visiting a neighbor v: if v is not visited (tree edge), we recursively compute low[v], then update low[u] = min(low[u], low[v]). If v is visited and v is not the parent of u (back edge), we update low[u] = min(low[u], disc[v]). This ensures low[u] captures the earliest discovery time accessible from u. This process propagates low values up the DFS tree, allowing articulation point detection. Time complexity is O(1) per edge update. Space is O(1) per vertex for low array. Connection to L2: Low values are essential for the articulation condition low[v] ≥ disc[u]. Common mistakes include updating low[u] with disc[v] instead of low[v] for tree edges, or not excluding the parent when checking back edges. Real code pattern: In dfs(u, parent): initialize low[u]=disc[u]. For each neighbor v: if not visited[v]: dfs(v, u); low[u]=min(low[u], low[v]). elif v != parent: low[u]=min(low[u], disc[v]).

examples.input: Vertex u with disc[u]=2, neighbors: child v with low[v]=1, back edge to w with disc[w]=0
examples.trace: Step 1: Start with low[u]=disc[u]=2. Step 2: Process child v: since v is not visited? Actually in this context, v is a child already processed, so low[v]=1. Update low[u]=min(2, 1)=1. Step 3: Process back edge to w: w is visited and w != parent (assuming parent is not w). Update low[u]=min(1, disc[w]=0)=0. Final low[u]=0.

examples.input: Vertex u with disc[u]=3, neighbors: child v with low[v]=4, no back edges
examples.trace: Step 1: low[u]=disc[u]=3. Step 2: Process child v: low[v]=4. Update low[u]=min(3, 4)=3. Step 3: No back edges. Final low[u]=3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — DFS Traversal on an Undirected Graph
ladders.desc: Perform a depth-first search to visit all vertices in an undirected graph.
ladders.explanation: DFS is a fundamental graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. For an undirected graph, we start at a vertex, mark it as visited, then recursively visit all its unvisited neighbors. The intuition is to systematically explore the graph to ensure all vertices are visited, which is essential for algorithms like Tarjan's that require full traversal. In pseudocode: def dfs(u): visited[u] = True; for each neighbor v of u: if not visited[v]: dfs(v). This ensures each vertex is processed once, and each edge is considered twice (once from each endpoint). Time complexity is O(V+E) as each vertex and edge is processed. Space complexity is O(V) for visited array and recursion stack. Connection to L3: DFS provides the framework for computing discovery times and low values. Common mistakes include not marking vertices as visited before recursion (leading to infinite loops), or not handling disconnected graphs (requiring DFS from each unvisited vertex). Real code pattern: Initialize visited array of size V with False. For each vertex u from 0 to V-1: if not visited[u]: dfs(u). In dfs(u): visited[u]=True; for v in adj[u]: if not visited[v]: dfs(v).

examples.input: Graph with vertices 0,1,2: edges 0-1, 1-2. Start DFS from 0.
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at vertex 0, mark visited[0]=True. Neighbors: 1. Step 2: Vertex 1 not visited, call dfs(1). Mark visited[1]=True. Neighbors: 0 and 2. Vertex 0 already visited, skip. Vertex 2 not visited. Step 3: Call dfs(2). Mark visited[2]=True. Neighbors: 1, already visited. Return to dfs(1), then to dfs(0). Visited order: 0,1,2.

examples.input: Graph with vertices 0,1,2: edges 0-1, 0-2. Start DFS from 0.
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at vertex 0, mark visited[0]=True. Neighbors: 1 and 2. Suppose we process neighbor 1 first. Step 2: Vertex 1 not visited, call dfs(1). Mark visited[1]=True. Neighbors: 0, already visited. Return to vertex 0. Step 3: Process next neighbor 2: not visited, call dfs(2). Mark visited[2]=True. Neighbors: 0, already visited. Return to vertex 0. Visited order: 0,1,2. If processing order changes, order might be 0,2,1.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph Representation and Basic Terminology
ladders.desc: Understand undirected graphs, vertices, edges, and adjacency lists.
ladders.explanation: A graph is a collection of vertices (nodes) and edges connecting pairs of vertices. In an undirected graph, edges have no direction; an edge between vertex u and v implies a two-way connection. Key terminology: vertex (point in the graph), edge (link between vertices), adjacency list (a common representation where each vertex has a list of its neighbors). For example, if vertices are 0,1,2 and edges are 0-1 and 1-2, the adjacency list is: 0: [1], 1: [0,2], 2: [1]. This representation is efficient for DFS as it allows quick access to neighbors. The intuition is that graphs model relationships, and traversal algorithms like DFS explore these relationships. Time and space for building adjacency list: O(V+E) time to process edges, O(V+E) space to store lists. Connection to L4: DFS operates on this graph representation. Common mistakes include assuming directed graphs, or incorrect indexing in adjacency lists. Real code pattern: Initialize adj as a list of empty lists for V vertices. For each edge (u,v): adj[u].append(v); adj[v].append(u) for undirected graph.

examples.trace: Step 1: Create empty lists for 3 vertices: adj = [[], [], []]. Step 2: Process edge 0-1: append 1 to adj[0] → adj[0]=[1]; append 0 to adj[1] → adj[1]=[0]. Step 3: Process edge 1-2: append 2 to adj[1] → adj[1]=[0,2]; append 1 to adj[2] → adj[2]=[1]. Final: adj[0]=[1], adj[1]=[0,2], adj[2]=[1].
